hook: You already know how to evaluate arithmetic expressions like $3 + 5 = 8$ — they either equal a specific number or they don't. But what about statements like "It is raining" or "7 is greater than 3"? These aren't equations you can calculate, yet they still have a definite truth value. How can we build a rigorous mathematical system around everyday language, and what happens when we combine two statements with words like "and" or "or"?
concept: A proposition is a declarative statement that is either true or false, but not both. Formally, a proposition $p$ has a truth value drawn from the set $\{T, F\}$ (or equivalently $\{1, 0\}$). Logical connectives — negation $\neg$, conjunction $\land$, disjunction $\lor$, conditional $\rightarrow$, and biconditional $\leftrightarrow$ — combine propositions into compound propositions whose truth value depends entirely on the truth values of their components.
anatomy: Every compound proposition has three identifiable parts. First, the atomic propositions (also called propositional variables), typically labelled $p$, $q$, $r$, which are the simplest statements with no internal logical structure. Second, the connective itself — for example, $\land$ (AND) requires both operands to be true, $\lor$ (OR) requires at least one to be true, and $\neg$ (NOT) flips a single truth value. Third, the resulting compound proposition, such as $p \land q$, which is a new statement whose truth value is completely determined by a truth table applied to its components.

worked_example.expression_latex: p: \text{"It is sunny."} \quad q: \text{"I will go running."}
worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying and labelling our atomic propositions. Giving each simple statement a variable name like $p$ and $q$ is essential because it lets us manipulate the logical structure without worrying about the English wording at every step.

worked_example.expression_latex: p \land q: \text{"It is sunny AND I will go running."}
worked_example.description_latex: We form the conjunction $p \land q$, which is true only when both $p$ is true and $q$ is true. If it is sunny but you decide to stay home, the conjunction is false — both conditions must hold simultaneously for $\land$ to give $T$.

worked_example.expression_latex: \begin{array}{cc|c} p & q & p \land q \\ \hline T & T & T \\ T & F & F \\ F & T & F \\ F & F & F \end{array}
worked_example.description_latex: We build the truth table by exhausting all four combinations of truth values for $p$ and $q$. Notice that only the first row yields $T$, confirming that conjunction is the most restrictive of the basic connectives — both inputs must be true.

worked_example.expression_latex: \neg p: \text{"It is NOT sunny."} \quad \text{Truth value of } \neg p \text{ when } p = T \text{ is } F
worked_example.description_latex: Negation $\neg p$ simply inverts the truth value of $p$: when $p$ is true, $\neg p$ is false, and vice versa. This is the only unary connective we study — it takes exactly one proposition rather than two.

worked_example.expression_latex: p \lor q: \text{true when at least one of } p, q \text{ is } T
worked_example.description_latex: The disjunction $p \lor q$ is false only in the one case where both $p$ and $q$ are false. In all three remaining rows of a truth table for $p$ and $q$, $p \lor q$ evaluates to $T$, making disjunction much more permissive than conjunction.

partial_example.expression_latex: p: \text{"The number is even."} \quad q: \text{"The number is greater than 10."}
partial_example.description_latex: We are given two atomic propositions and we want to evaluate the compound proposition $\neg p \lor q$ for the specific number $12$. Begin by determining the truth values of $p$ and $q$ individually: $12$ is even, so $p = T$; $12 > 10$, so $q = T$.
Now we apply negation to $p$. Since $p$ is true, $\neg p$ flips to false. This intermediate step is crucial before combining with $q$ using disjunction.
Using $\neg p = F$ and $q = T$, evaluate $\neg p \lor q$ by applying the disjunction rule. Write out the substitution and state the final truth value of the compound proposition.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Is the sentence "Paris is the capital of France" a proposition? If so, state its truth value.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Yes, it is a proposition. Its truth value is } T.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Let } p = T \text{ and } q = F. \text{ Evaluate } p \lor q \text{ and } p \land q.
practice_problems.answer_latex: p \lor q = T, \quad p \land q = F

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Let } p = F \text{ and } q = F. \text{ Evaluate } \neg p \land \neg q.
\text{Construct the full truth table for } (p \lor q) \land \neg p.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \begin{array}{cc|c} p & q & (p \lor q) \land \neg p \\ \hline T & T & F \\ T & F & F \\ F & T & T \\ F & F & F \end{array}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A student says: "I will pass the exam or I will not study." Let } p = \text{"I will pass the exam"} \text{ and } q = \text{"I will study."}  \text{ Express the sentence symbolically, then evaluate it when } p = F \text{ and } q = T.
practice_problems.answer_latex:
p \lor 
eg q; \quad 
eg q = F, \quad p \lor 
eg q = F \lor F = F

common_mistakes: Students often treat disjunction $p \lor q$ as exclusive OR, believing it is false when both $p$ and $q$ are true. In standard logic, $\lor$ is inclusive OR and is true whenever at least one operand is true, including the case where both are true. Always check the $T,T$ row first to remind yourself of this.

When negating a compound proposition, students frequently write $
eg p \land q$ to mean 'negate the whole thing $p \land q$', forgetting that $
eg(p \land q)$ is different from $
eg p \land q$. The negation symbol applies only to what is immediately to its right, so use parentheses explicitly: $
eg(p \land q)$ means the entire conjunction is negated.

Students sometimes classify questions ('Is it raining?') or commands ('Close the door.') as propositions because they are sentences. A proposition must be a declarative statement with a definite truth value; questions and commands have neither $T$ nor $F$, so they are not propositions. Always ask yourself: can this sentence be true or false?
untested_variants: Evaluating the conditional $p \rightarrow q$ and biconditional $p \leftrightarrow q$, including the counterintuitive case where a false hypothesis makes $p \rightarrow q$ vacuously true.
Determining whether a compound proposition is a tautology, contradiction, or contingency by constructing a full truth table and inspecting every row.
Translating complex English sentences with nested connectives (e.g., 'If it is raining and cold, then I will stay home or read') into symbolic form with correct use of parentheses for precedence.
Using operator precedence rules ($\neg$ before $\land$ before $\lor$ before $\rightarrow$ before $\leftrightarrow$) to parse an expression like $p \lor q \land \neg r$ without added parentheses.
